nativeRange.habitat: The Threadfin Lookdown inhabits shallow coastal waters, estuaries, and sandy bays. Juveniles tend to be found in estuarine areas and along sandy beaches.

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming small crabs, shrimps, small fish, and worms in the wild. In captivity, has been known to accept brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, krill, squid, and small feeder fish. Generally considered a picky eater, especially when newly introduced; frequent small feedings are generally advised, and dietary variety is important for long-term health.

imageCandidates.notes: MatthiasKabel; 4070x2713. Two lookdowns together (schooling species), front fish sharp and vibrant blue, showing the disc-shaped compressed body, elongated dorsal/anal threadfin filaments and shimmering silver-blue flanks.
Amada44; 4819x3347. Tack-sharp lone specimen against a dark background, clearly showing the steep 'looking down' facial profile, iridescent silver-blue disc body and trailing dorsal filament.
Greg Hume; 1024x683. Sharp side profile with strong iridescent silver-violet-orange sheen and long anal/dorsal threadfin filaments, whole fish in frame.

summary: The Threadfin Lookdown is a striking, disc-shaped jack from the western Atlantic, known for its shimmering silver-blue flanks and elongated dorsal and anal fin filaments. Generally considered an advanced-level fish best kept in very large systems with plenty of open swimming room.
careNotes:
The Threadfin Lookdown (Selene vomer) is a pelagic jack requiring a very large aquarium — generally advised to be at least 1500 liters (approximately 400 gallons) to accommodate its active schooling behavior and potential adult size of up to 49 cm. It tends to thrive when kept in groups of at least 3, and feeding together has been reported to encourage natural schooling behavior and reduce competition. Newly introduced specimens tend to be picky eaters; frequent small meals of varied meaty foods (krill, mysis, squid, small live or frozen items) are generally advised to get them feeding reliably.

The species has extremely thin, delicate skin that is easily damaged during capture, transport, or netting; care is strongly advised when handling. Its highly reflective, shimmering flanks can be startling to tankmates.

While generally peaceful toward similarly-sized fish, it has been known to consume smaller fish and crustaceans that fit in its mouth. It should not be housed with fin nippers or aggressive species, as its long trailing fins are vulnerable.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (19 in / 48.3 cm), temperament, care level. Tank minimum listed as 200 gallons (757 L) vs LiveAquaria's 500 gallons (1893 L) — averaged with other sources to ~1550 L.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked maximum size (48.3 cm TL), native range (Western Atlantic), family (Carangidae), order (Carangiformes), temperature range (11.8–27.9°C wild). Wild temperature range is much broader than aquarium recommendations; aquarium-specific range averaged from hobby sources.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked grouping (keep in groups of 3+), tank size (2000 L minimum), diet, reef compatibility. Size listed as 48 cm.

additional.name: EshaLabs Aquapedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (24–27°C), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), pH (7.5–8.5), grouping (4+ recommended), diet notes. Size listed as 50 cm. Tank minimum 380 L (100 gal) — likely inadequate for full-grown specimens; not heavily weighted for minimum tank calculation.
